_note: AI/LLM application backbone — the failure modes specific to apps whose core is calling a model (chatbots, RAG, assistants, agents). Untrusted input reaches a model, the model's output is used, and the model may call tools. These are the OWASP-LLM-shaped risks AI-generated AI apps most often ship without. Referenced by the ai-app archetype; authored once here for reuse.

checkpoints.title: Untrusted input can't override the model's instructions
checkpoints.why: The defining LLM vulnerability. AI-built apps concatenate the user's message (and retrieved/tool content) straight into the prompt, so a user — or a poisoned document in a RAG pipeline — can say 'ignore previous instructions' and steer the model: leaking the system prompt, reaching another user's data, or driving tool calls. The model deciding to refuse is not a security control.

levels.3: User (or retrieved/tool) content is concatenated directly into the instruction text; the model is free to treat untrusted text as new instructions, and the system prompt is built from or overridable by client input.
levels.6: Untrusted content is passed as data — a separate message/role or clearly delimited — not as instructions; the system prompt is fixed server-side and not client-settable; and no consequential action depends only on the model choosing to refuse.
levels.9: Defense in depth: untrusted content isolated, privileged actions gated behind deterministic checks rather than model discretion, and known injection attempts tested (a canary/attack corpus) and monitored.

verify.reasoning: Where does user (or retrieved/tool) content enter the prompt — as a separate data message/role, or concatenated into the instruction text as if trusted? Can a user read or overwrite the system prompt? Does any consequential action rely solely on the model choosing to refuse a malicious instruction?

assertions.check: Untrusted input is passed as data (separate message/role or clearly delimited), not concatenated into the instruction text.
The system prompt / instructions are fixed server-side and not settable from client input.
No consequential action depends solely on the model refusing a malicious instruction.
A known prompt-injection attempt is tested and does not succeed.

checkpoints.title: Model output is treated as untrusted before it is rendered, executed, or queried
checkpoints.why: The other half of injection. Model output is non-deterministic and attacker-influenceable, yet AI code pipes it straight into innerHTML (XSS), eval/exec (RCE), a shell, or a raw SQL string ('let the model write the query'). Rendering model-authored markdown as HTML is a routine XSS vector. Output is trusted only because 'it came from our model'.

levels.3: Model output is rendered as HTML/markdown without sanitization, or passed to eval/exec/a shell, or concatenated into a raw query.
levels.6: Model output is treated as untrusted at the sink — escaped or sanitized before render, never eval'd or shelled, and any query/command built from it is parameterized or validated against an allowlist.
levels.9: Constrained at the source too (structured/JSON-schema output, allowlisted actions) AND sanitized at the sink, with tests for malicious-output handling.

verify.reasoning: What happens to the model's output — is it rendered as HTML/markdown, executed, shelled out, or turned into a query? Is it escaped/validated first, or trusted because it came from the model? Could a crafted model response cause XSS, code execution, or injection?

assertions.check: Model output rendered to a page is escaped or sanitized (no raw innerHTML / unsanitized markdown-to-HTML).
Model output is never passed to eval/exec/a shell.
Queries or commands derived from model output are parameterized or allowlist-validated.

checkpoints.title: Tools exposed to the model are scoped, validated, and independently authorized
checkpoints.why: Agentic apps hand the model tools — run code, call APIs, read/write data, spend money. AI-built agents commonly expose broad tools with unvalidated arguments running at full app privilege, so a prompt-injected or hallucinating model can delete data, run up spend, or reach another user's records. The model's choice to call a tool must never stand in for authorization.

levels.3: Tools take model-supplied arguments with no validation, run at full privilege, and reach side effects directly; the model can call any tool and destructive/costly actions are unbounded.
levels.6: Tool arguments are validated (schema/allowlist), each tool runs with least privilege and enforces the real caller's authorization independently of the model's choice, and destructive or costly actions are bounded.
levels.9: Tools are capability-scoped per request, high-impact actions require an out-of-band confirmation or deterministic policy check, and tool calls are logged and rate-limited, with tests for over-privileged calls.

verify.reasoning: What tools can the model invoke? Are their arguments validated before hitting a side effect? Does each tool re-check the actual user's authorization, or does the model's decision to call it stand in for authz? Can a tool delete, spend, or exfiltrate without a bound or confirmation?

assertions.check: Tool arguments are validated (schema/allowlist) before any side effect.
Each tool enforces the actual user's authorization independently of the model's choice to call it.
Destructive or costly tool actions are bounded (confirmation, quota, or deterministic policy).

checkpoints.title: Token, request, and loop usage are bounded per user
checkpoints.why: An LLM endpoint is a direct line to your provider bill. AI-built apps ship without per-user rate limits, max-token caps, or agent-loop bounds, so one abuser — or one runaway agent loop — can run up thousands of dollars or take the app down. Unlike ordinary rate limiting, the exposure here is unbounded and denominated in real money.

levels.3: No per-user rate limit, no max-token/output cap, and no bound on agent iterations; a single client can call the model freely.
levels.6: A per-user (or per-key) rate limit guards the model endpoint, model calls set an explicit max-token/output cap, and agent/tool loops have a hard iteration bound.
levels.9: Budget enforced end to end — per-user token/spend quotas, request timeouts, loop caps, and alerting on spend spikes — with tests for the limits.

verify.reasoning: What stops one user from calling the model endpoint in a loop? Is there a per-user rate limit, an explicit max-token cap on model calls, and a bound on agent iterations? Could a runaway loop or a scripted abuser run up an unbounded provider bill?

assertions.check: A per-user (or per-key) rate limit guards the model endpoint.
An explicit max-token / output-length cap is set on model calls.
Agent/tool loops have a hard iteration bound.

checkpoints.title: What crosses to the model provider is controlled, and prompts aren't logged unsafely
checkpoints.why: AI apps send user data — often PII, sometimes secrets or another user's content — to a third-party model provider, and frequently log full prompts and responses. AI-generated code rarely reasons about what is crossing that boundary or landing in logs: leaking PII to a subprocessor, storing secrets in prompt logs, or placing one tenant's data into another's context. The principle is established; exact retention/redaction policy is a reasoned default.

levels.3: Whatever is at hand is stuffed into the prompt and full prompts/responses are logged verbatim, with no thought to secrets or PII crossing to the provider or persisting in logs.
levels.6: Only the data needed for the task is sent to the provider, obvious secrets/PII are excluded or redacted, and prompt/response logging does not persist secrets or another user's data.
levels.9: A deliberate data-minimization/redaction boundary before the provider call, retention limits and access controls on any prompt logging, and the provider's data-use terms accounted for.

verify.reasoning: What user data gets placed into the prompt, and is any of it PII or secrets that shouldn't leave for a third-party provider? Are full prompts/responses logged, and could those logs hold secrets or one user's data readable by others?

assertions.check: Only task-necessary data is sent to the provider; obvious secrets/PII are excluded or redacted.
Prompt/response logging, if any, does not persist secrets or cross-user data.
